feedback.text: One of the most serious and under-regulated issues in digital consumer rights today is the ability of publishers and platforms to revoke access to purchased digital content without offering alternatives, refunds, or long-term solutions. This is especially problematic in the gaming industry, where users often pay 6080 (or far more through microtransactions), only to lose access entirely when servers are shut down. Even single-player games are increasingly tied to mandatory online connectivity, making them completely unplayable after shutdowns. Recent examples include The Crew (Ubisoft), which was permanently disabled despite being sold as a full-price game, and Overwatch (Blizzard), where players lost access to the original game when it was replaced by Overwatch 2. Digital content is being treated not as a product, but as a license even when full-price purchases are made. Today, purchasing a physical box often results in receiving only a digital activation key. Meanwhile, platforms like Steam explicitly state that accounts are non-transferable and may be terminated upon the users death, effectively denying inheritance. This is equivalent to buying a book or a film and having it revoked or deleted by the publisher years later. If digital content is presented with terms like Buy, Pay, or Subscribe, it should guarantee meaningful, long-term access. If something is temporary, it should be clearly marked as Rent. These issues extend well beyond games. Streaming platforms like Netflix, Spotify, or Xbox Game Pass regularly remove content without notice due to licensing changes. Consumers can invest time, emotional attachment, or even money in these platforms, only to see favorite films, shows, or songs disappear overnight. Platforms should be legally required to offer a purchase and download option for content allowing consumers to secure access if they so choose. In Slovenia, for example, a conflict between a music label and an artist (Raay Production and Nika Zorjan) led to the complete removal of the artists back catalogue from all online services, depriving listeners of access to legally released work and erasing digital culture with a single corporate decision. Consumers must have the right to continued access to their purchases. If a games servers are shut down, publishers should be required to provide an offline mode or enable the community to host private servers across all platforms, including consoles. Console manufacturers must also ensure their systems support such scenarios. For other types of content like music, movies, software, or e-books, platforms should offer downloadable files in all quality tiers (HD, 4K, lossless, etc.) for at least 10 years after delisting or removal. If this is not feasible, the consumer must have the right to request a full refund of all amounts ever spent on that title. Introducing these requirements would not harm the digital economy it would enhance trust, responsibility, and transparency. Publishers would need to plan for End-of-Life (EoL) support early in development, resulting in cleaner architecture and better design practices. This could reduce digital planned obsolescence, promote long-term engagement, and support more resilient ecosystems. In fact, we might return to more open, creative eras like those seen in Minecraft, FiveM, or MTA: San Andreas, where community-driven servers extended a games life by decades and empowered local ownership. Finally, the legal framework around intellectual property must evolve. Many developers hesitate to allow modding or private servers out of fear that they might lose copyright protections. EU legislation should clearly distinguish between official IP and permitted community-based preservation efforts after a products official retirement. Digital preservation and consumer rights can coexist with copyright protections and they must, if we hope to build a fairer digital future.
